metadata.title: Why 'Make This Better' Is Not a Prompt

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The methods-section story landed hard — a clean rewrite that was wrong for the journal, the audience, and the argument, with no signal anything failed because 'better' was never defined. Help me stop shipping vague frames: (1) take my own 'improve my methods section' request and expand it into the six inspectable components; (2) pay special attention to the three usually missing — source material, constraints, and evaluation criteria; (3) show me a before-and-after so I can see how naming the venue and reviewer changes what the model completes.

props.command: The methods-section story landed hard — a clean rewrite that was wrong for the journal, the audience, and the argument, with no signal anything failed because 'better' was never defined. Help me stop shipping vague frames: (1) take my own 'improve my methods section' request and expand it into the six inspectable components; (2) pay special attention to the three usually missing — source material, constraints, and evaluation criteria; (3) show me a before-and-after so I can see how naming the venue and reviewer changes what the model completes.

beats.narration_text: A researcher sent Claude one line: 'Can you improve my methods section?' Two minutes later she had a polished rewrite. It was wrong for her journal, her audience, and the argument she was making.

production_viz.label: vague request — fluent wrong output
production_viz.mechanic: a chat bubble (slate border) shows 'Can you improve my methods section?' above a TEAL chip 'POLISHED REWRITE' with a CRIMSON tag 'WRONG FOR HER JOURNAL'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] vague request — polished but wrong output

beats.narration_text: The prose was clean and the structure was tight. There was no signal in the output that anything had gone wrong.
beats.scene_description: researcher at a laptop reading a polished document printout with a puzzled expression; she expected improvement but the result is mismatched; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, researcher at desk reading a polished document printout with a puzzled expression, laptop open, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, no text legible, collage aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] researcher reading a polished but wrong rewrite

beats.narration_text: The researcher asked for improvement and got it — by Claude's definition. Her definition was never stated. Why did a better prompt require anything more than describing the task?

card.copy: Why did 'improve this' produce the wrong improvement?

beats.narration_text: You might expect that a capable language model knows what 'better' means — that it reads the document, infers the context, and applies the right standard automatically.

production_viz.label: naive model: capable model infers the right standard
production_viz.mechanic: chip 'VAGUE REQUEST' (crimson) -> arrow -> chip 'RIGHT IMPROVEMENT' (teal) with label 'the naive expectation — model infers context automatically'

beats.narration_text: A language model generates fluent text by completing the frame the prompt implies. A vague frame — 'make this better' — is completed with the model's best generic standard: clean prose, shorter sentences, standard academic structure. It has no idea what venue, what reviewer, what argument you are trying to make.

production_viz.label: frame completion: vague prompt -> generic standard applied
production_viz.mechanic: left: VAGUE PROMPT chip (crimson) with sub-label 'no criteria / no source constraints / no scope'; right: GENERIC STANDARD chip (crimson) with sub-label 'clean prose / shorter sentences / standard structure'; arrow between; both wrong because not specific to actual situation

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] frame completion — vague prompt triggers generic fill

beats.narration_text: A prompt is a specification of work. A specification tells the collaborator four things: what to do, what to use, what to avoid, and what the finished work should look like. When those are absent, the model fills them with its best guess — fluent and wrong.

card.copy: A prompt is a specification of work.

beats.narration_text: Six components make a prompt inspectable: the task, the context, the source material, the constraints, the output format, and the evaluation criteria. The three most commonly missing are source material, constraints, and evaluation criteria — the parts that define what 'better' means for your situation.

production_viz.label: six components of a specification
production_viz.mechanic: six chips in two columns: TASK, CONTEXT, SOURCE MATERIAL, CONSTRAINTS, OUTPUT FORMAT, EVALUATION CRITERIA — last three highlighted in crimson (commonly missing)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] six specification components — the three missing ones

beats.narration_text: Priya asks 'Can you improve my methods section?' — polished rewrite, wrong audience. Rohan specifies: evaluate for a CHI venue, flag causal overclaims, diagnosis before rewriting, no new citations. Same task. Same model. The specified prompt returns something she can inspect and disagree with.

production_viz.label: Priya vs Rohan: same task, different specifications
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: PRIYA (crimson) 'Can you improve my methods section?' -> POLISHED REWRITE chip (teal-looking but crimson tag 'wrong for CHI venue'); ROHAN (teal) 6-component spec -> DIAGNOSIS chip (teal) + REVISION PLAN chip (teal) + 'inspectable'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Priya vs Rohan — vague vs specified

beats.narration_text: The practice move: before sending any Claude prompt, ask which of the six components is missing. Write down what 'better' means in your specific context. State the evaluation criteria explicitly — what would a good output get right that a mediocre one misses?

production_viz.label: the specification check — before sending any prompt
production_viz.mechanic: vertical list of three teal chips: 'WHAT DOES BETTER MEAN HERE?' / 'WHAT SOURCE MATERIAL IS VALID?' / 'WHAT MUST IT NOT DO?'; labeled 'the specification check'

beats.narration_text: The check: can you tell from the output whether Claude answered YOUR question or a reasonable generic version of it? If the output is inspectable against your criteria, the specification worked. If not, the missing component is the one you cannot check.

card.copy: Can you inspect the output against your own criteria?

beats.narration_text: The wrong prompt costs more than a bad search query — because the output is fluent. A bad search returns nothing. A bad prompt returns something polished and confident that passes review without triggering a check.

card.copy: A bad prompt returns something polished and wrong.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the asymmetry — fluent output masks underspecification

beats.narration_text: For the researcher: the prompt is not the request. The prompt is the work order. Task. Context. Source material. Constraints. Output format. Evaluation criteria. State what 'better' means before Claude decides for you.

production_viz.label: the work order — six components in sequence
production_viz.mechanic: six chips arranged vertically: TASK / CONTEXT / SOURCE MATERIAL / CONSTRAINTS / OUTPUT FORMAT / EVALUATION CRITERIA — all teal, each with a brief label

beats.narration_text: The specification is what makes the output yours to own — not Claude's generic best guess. Define better before you ask.
beats.scene_description: researcher at desk writing on a notepad before opening her laptop, deliberate and focused, preparing her criteria before drafting a prompt; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment
beats.image_prompt: B13, researcher at desk writing notes on a notepad before opening laptop, deliberate focused expression, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, no text legible, collage aesthetic

beats.narration_text: A prompt that says 'make this better' is not a failure of the model. It is a failure of specification. The model did what you asked. You asked for something that could not produce the output you wanted — because you never said what you wanted.

card.copy: The model did what you asked. You asked for the wrong thing.

beats.narration_text: A prompt is a specification. State what to do, what to use, what to avoid, and what success looks like. Then you can inspect the output — and know if it worked.

card.copy: State what success looks like before Claude decides.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. A prompt that says 'make this better' is not a failure of the model. It is a failure of specification. The model did what you asked. You asked for something that could not produce the output you wanted — because you never said what you wanted. A prompt is a specification. State what to do, what to use, what to avoid, and what success looks like. Then you can inspect the output — and know if it worked.

props.artifactHeading: Why 'Make This Better' Is Not a Prompt
artifactLines: The specification is what makes the output yours to own — not Claude's generic best guess.
A prompt that says 'make this better' is not a failure of the model.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why 'Make This Better' Is Not a Prompt] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
